questions.explanation: $1/\phi = \phi - 1 \approx 0.618$. A clean fact: the decimal expansion of $1/\phi$ matches $\phi$'s after the first digit.

questions.promptTex: \text{Compute the ratio } F_{10}/F_9 \text{ (where } F_{10} = 55, F_9 = 34). \\ \text{ Round to 4 decimals.}

hints: The ratio of consecutive Fibonacci numbers converges.
questions.explanation: $F_{n+1}/F_n \to \phi$ — a key connection between Fibonacci and $\phi$.

choices: A rectangle inscribed in a circle.
hints: Cutting a square off one end yields another golden rectangle.
questions.explanation: A rectangle with long/short ratio $\phi$. Self-similar under cutting off a square.

questions.prompt: Why does $\phi$ arise in plant leaf arrangements (phyllotaxis)?
choices: Because rotating each new leaf by an angle proportional to $\phi$ (the 'golden angle') produces the most efficient packing — no two leaves overshadow each other.
Because $\phi$ is the maximum sunlight angle.
Coincidence — $\phi$ doesn't really appear in plants.
hints: $\phi$ is the 'most irrational' number — best for avoiding lined-up leaves.
Plants evolved this for sunlight efficiency.
questions.explanation: $\phi$ is the most irrational number (hardest to approximate by rationals). Rotating each leaf by $\phi$ of a turn avoids any periodic overlap, maximizing sunlight exposure for all leaves.

steps.text: **Approach.** $\phi$ is the 'most irrational' number — best for avoiding lined-up leaves.
Plants evolved this for sunlight efficiency.

**Solution.** $\phi$ is the most irrational number (hardest to approximate by rationals). Rotating each leaf by $\phi$ of a turn avoids any periodic overlap, maximizing sunlight exposure for all leaves.
